---
title: "Sparse Autoencoders"
author: Kris Sankaran
date: 2026-04-20
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    fig-align: center
    code-overflow: wrap
execute:
    echo: true
    message: false
    warning: false
    cache: false
    eval: true
---

::: {style="display: none;"}
$$
\newcommand{\bs}[1]{\mathbf{#1}}
\newcommand{\reals}{\mathbb{R}}
\newcommand{\widebar}[1]{\overline{#1}}
\newcommand{\E}{\mathbb{E}}
\newcommand{\indic}[1]{\mathbb{1}\left\{{#1}\right\}}
\newcommand{\Earg}[1]{\mathbb{E}\left[{#1}\right]}
\newcommand{\Esubarg}[2]{\mathbb{E}_{#1}\left[{#2}\right]}
$$
:::

<style>
.purple { color: #7458d1ff; } /* pastel purple */
.orange { color: #fca020; } /* pastel orange */
.green { color: #3bbe67ff; } /* pastel green */
.darkblue { color: #4a9ceaff; } /* pastel dark blue */
.pink { color: #ee6ec3ff; } /* pastel pink */
</style>

```{r}
#| label: fig-setup
#| echo: false
library(tidyverse)
library(reticulate)
theme_set(theme_classic() + theme(panel.border= element_rect(fill = NA, linewidth = .5)))
set.seed(2026)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-pysetup
#| echo: false
# includes the SHAP package. Can install it using,
# > conda env create -f stat479_week13_demo.yaml
# where the yaml file is located at: https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat479_notes/blob/master/notes/stat479_week13_demo.yaml
use_condaenv("stat479_week13_demo")
```

_[Readings](https://) [Code](https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat479_notes/blob/master/notes/15-sparse_autoencoders.qmd)_,  _[Conda Env](https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat479_notes/blob/master/notes/stat479_week13_demo.yaml)_

Items marked $^{\dagger}$ will not be tested.

## Setup

1. **Goal**. Discover what abstract features a large language model (LLM) $f$
has learned and use those to "steer" model outputs.

1. **Requirements**.

   - The method should be computationally scalable, because typical LLMs are
   trained on large datasets and have many parameters.

   - The annotation process should also be scalable, without requiring too much
   human labeling effort.

1. **Approach.** Learn a "dictionary" of interpretable directions
$\left(v_k\right)_{k = 1}^{K}$ in a layer's activation space, in the sense that
any observation activations $x_n$ can be reconstructed using a sparse
combination of those learned directions,
   $$
   x_n \approx \text{intercept} + \sum_{\text{ few } k} z_{nk} v_k
   $$
   We will steer the model predictions by increasing or decreasing the
   contribution of those directions.

1. _Motivation_

   - Concept analysis is confirmatory -- it requires manually-curated examples.
   We instead want an exploratory approach that helps us find relevant features
   automatically. Especially important in fairness/AI safety applications, where
   we might not be sure of problematic features in advance.
   - LLMs are generative, i.e. they can be used to simulate new data points
   rather than just being used to make predictions. It's not enough to associate
   concepts with predictions, we really need to control the generations, adding
   and removing concepts as needed.

1. _Preview._

   - The techniques in this note will allow us to discover non-obvious features. E.g.,
     ![One of the features has high score $z_{nk}$ on samples related to the Golden Gate Bridge. It activates across languages and data modalities.](figures/golden_gate.png){width=80%}
   - We'll be able to use those features to steer model output.
     ![We'll be able to use those features to "steer" the model output.](figures/golden_gate_steering.png){width=70%}

## Sparse Autoencoder Model

1. **Naive Approach 1 - Individual Neurons.** Some past work tried to describe
individual neurons by finding the examples that they gave the strongest
activations for, e.g., some neurons in lower layers act like edge detectors.

   ![Examples of neurons in a CNN that activate whenever they encounter particular edge orientations, from @Zeiler2014.](figures/zeiler_edge_detector.png){width=40%}

   This is not satisfactory in higher-layers, though. Individual neurons play
   multiple roles depending on their context, a phenomenon that's been called
   the "superposition hypothesis."

1. **Naive Approach 2 - Dimensionality Reduction.** We could apply a
dimensionality reduction method to the learned activations and then interpret
the resulting plots.

   ![@Johnson2016 applied a dimensionality-reduction method to activations from a language translation model. Each color in the left panel corresponds to a single sentence translated into many languages. The analysis shows that the model learns to align sentences with the same meaning regardless of language.](figures/multilingual_tsne.png)

   This is not so useful for understanding activations over larger datasets,
   like those used in LLM training.  LLMs are trained on wide swaths of the
   internet, yet their embedding dimensions are comparatively small. The Claude
   3 analysis from the reading doesn't disclose the dimension of the layer they
   analyzed, but the comparable layer in GPT-3 has 12K dimensions.  There are
   more than 12K "concepts" on the internet...

1. **Notation**. The main idea is to expand -- not reduce -- the
dimensionality of the activation space in a way that disentangles the distinct
components of the learned representations. To make this precise, we need some notation,

   - $x_n \in \reals^{D}$: The activations of sample $n$ at layer $l$.
   - $v_1, \dots, v_K$: Underlying features in layer $l$. These are called "atoms" in the dictionary learning literature and we set $K \gg D$.
   - $z_n \in \reals^{K}$: Mixing the atoms $v_k$ with weights $z_n$
   should closely approximate $x_n$. We try to make the $z_n$ sparse, so only a
   few atoms are relevant to any given sample.

   ![](figures/expansion_diagram.png)

1. **Architecture**. The sparse autoencoder (SAE) model relates $x_n$ with $v_k$
and $z_n$. The form used in the reading is^[This is not exactly the standard
form of the dictionary learning objective, but we will ignore the (minor)
differences @Yun2021 @Olshausen1996.].
   - Encoder
   \begin{align}
   z_n = g\left(W x_n + b\right)
   \end{align}
    where $g\left(u\right) = u\indic{u > 0}$ is the ReLU function.
   - Decoder
   \begin{align}
   \hat{x}_n = V z_n + c
   \end{align}

   ![](figures/encoder_decoder_matrices.png)

   _Exercise: In the reading, what choices of $K$ are considered?_

1. Expanding the matrix multiplication in the decoder, we see that,
\begin{align}
x_n \approx \hat{x}_n = \text{intercept} + \sum_{k = 1}^{K} z_{nk} v_{k}
\end{align}
where we use $v_k$ to represent the $k^{th}$ column of $V$. We summarize $x_n$ as
a mixture of unobserved features $v_k$, and the nonnegative mixing weights are
$z_{nk}$.

   _Exercise: What are the dimensionalities of $x_n, \hat{x}_n, z_{nk}$, and $v_k$?_

   _Exercise: Why are the weights $z_{nk}$ nonnegative?_

1. In code, this looks like,

   ```{python}
class SAE(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, D, K):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = nn.Linear(D, K)
        self.decoder = nn.Linear(K, D, bias=True)

    def forward(self, x):
        z = F.relu(self.encoder(x))
        x_hat = self.decoder(z)
        return x_hat, z
   ```

1. We need to learn the parameters $W \in \reals^{K \times D}, V \in \reals^{D
\times K}, b \in \reals^{K}, c \in \reals^{D}$. We use the objective function,

   \begin{align}
   \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n = 1}^{N}\left[\|x_n - \hat{x}_n\|_{2}^{2} + \lambda \sum_{k} z_{nk} \|v_k\|_{2}\right].
   \end{align}
   Notice that the first term depends on both the encoder and decoder parameters,
   \begin{align}
    \|x_n - \hat{x}_n\|_{2}^{2} &= \|x_n - Vz_n - c\|_{2}^{2} \\
    &= \|x_n - V g\left(W x_n + b\right) - c\|_2^2
   \end{align}

   In code, this is,

   ```{python}
def sae_loss(x, x_hat, z, decoder, lambd):
    recon = F.mse_loss(x, x_hat)
    col_norms = decoder.weight.norm(dim=0)  # ||v_k||_2 for each k
    sparsity = (z * col_norms).sum(dim=1).mean()
    return recon + lambd * sparsity
   ```

1. It's not obvious, but the penalty is very related to the lasso. Since
$z_{nk}$ and $\|v_k\|_2$ are always nonnegative, the penalty is equivalent to,
\begin{align}
\lambda \sum_{k} \left|\alpha_k\right|
\end{align}
where $\alpha_k = z_{nk}\|v_k\|_2$. I.e., this is an $\ell^{1}$ penalty on the
product between the length of the latent features and the mixing weights. This
encourages the product to be exactly zero, just like the lasso encouraged
coordinates $\beta_j = 0$. The consequence is that the sum $\sum_{k} z_{nk}
v_{k}$ only includes a small number of nonzero $z_{nk}$, despite $K$ being
large.

1. A basis like $v_1, \dots, v_K$ where $K > D$ is called _overcomplete_.
Combined with sparsity of $z_{nk}$, this can be used to approximate more complex
geometric structures than linear dimensionality reduction.

   ![](figures/representation_capacity.png)

   _Exercise: Compare and contrast the $v_k$ in this equation with the concept vectors $v_{c}^{l}$ from the Concept Activation Vectors notes._

   _Exercise: What is the pattern of zeros in $z_{n}$ for the samples labeled $A, B$, and $C$? What is the relationship between the $z_{n}$ for the point labeled $B$ vs. $C$?_

**Scaling Laws**

## Discovery and Control

**Feature Survey**

**Steering**
